context.description: The author argues that archetypes and the symbolic world point to an active higher will or superself beyond ordinary waking consciousness. They claim conscious control cannot fully grasp the laws needed to shape large symbolic narratives like the hero's journey, and connect this to cognition, social conflict, and human self-blindness.
normalization_notes: Source language is German; summary and tags translated to English.
No chapter heading visible; page-level prose extract normalized as chapter text.

rows.paragraph_text: Archetypes and the symbolic world imply a higher will within the self that exceeds ordinary waking awareness.
rows.text_content: Archetypes and the symbolic world imply a higher will within the self that exceeds ordinary waking awareness.

rows.paragraph_text: Conscious control is said to be unable to fully understand the laws needed to form large symbolic arcs such as the hero's journey.
rows.text_content: Conscious control is said to be unable to fully understand the laws needed to form large symbolic arcs such as the hero's journey.

rows.paragraph_text: The argument extends to new models of reality, cognition, social conflict, and human self-blindness.
rows.text_content: The argument extends to new models of reality, cognition, social conflict, and human self-blindness.

rows.paragraph_text: An active will is justified as an unseen cognitive process rooted in deep evolutionary history.
rows.text_content: An active will is justified as an unseen cognitive process rooted in deep evolutionary history.
